items.mistake: Counting the input and output layers as part of network depth.
items.fix: Define depth as the number of hidden layers only. A network input → 64 → 32 → output has depth 2, not 4.
items.why: Papers, frameworks, and interview questions use hidden-layer count for depth; mixing definitions makes capacity comparisons meaningless.
items.example:
```
Architecture: 784 -> 128 -> 64 -> 10
Correct depth: 2 (two hidden layers of width 128 and 64)
Wrong depth: 4 (counting input and output tiers)
```

items.mistake: Confusing layer width with the number of weight parameters in the whole network.
items.fix: Width is neurons in one layer. Total parameters depend on all layer pairs: sum of (d_in * d_out + d_out) for each dense connection.
items.why: A single wide layer can have more parameters than several narrow layers, or vice versa — width alone does not tell you model size.
items.example: Hidden width 512 after 784 inputs: 784*512 + 512 = 401,920 weights in that layer alone — much larger than a 784→64 layer (50,240 weights).

items.mistake: Assuming each neuron only connects to one neuron in the next layer.
items.fix: In a fully connected layer, every neuron in layer L connects to every neuron in layer L-1. The connectivity is all-to-all, implemented as one dense matrix.
items.why: Sparse one-to-one wiring would be a different architecture (e.g. grouped or convolutional), not a standard dense MLP layer.
items.example: Hidden layer with 3 neurons after 4 inputs uses a 4x3 weight matrix W — 12 weights, not 3.
